Projects

P.01 Undergraduate Research In Progress

Quadcopter Flight Control System

GoAero Competition · MATLAB Simulink
Currently developing a physics-based quadcopter flight control system in MATLAB Simulink as part of an undergraduate research project for the GoAero competition. The project involves building a full drone plant model in Simulink Simscape by importing CAD geometry and extracting mass and inertia properties to simulate realistic vehicle dynamics and evaluate controller performance before physical implementation.

Upon completion of the team's tiltrotor aircraft design, a flight control system for that configuration will be designed and implemented — introducing the additional complexity of variable thrust vectoring and transitional flight modes.
Tools & Frameworks
MATLABSimulinkSimscapeCAD Import6DOF ModelPixhawk
Key Work
  • Physics-based 6DOF drone plant model in Simscape
  • CAD geometry import for mass & inertia extraction
  • Realistic vehicle dynamics simulation loop
  • Flight controller performance evaluation
  • Future: tiltrotor FCS design & implementation
// Simulation Model & Hardware
Simulink Simscape 6DOF plant model — 4 propellers, airframe, NED frame & ground contact
Physical quadcopter — Pixhawk flight controller, 915 MHz telemetry, GPS & power module
P.02 Aerospace Structures MAE 372 — Lab 5

Stiffened Aluminum Panel Design

Structural Analysis · SolidWorks FEA · Experimental Testing
Designed, fabricated, and tested a riveted aluminum stiffened panel to sustain a 3,000 lbf compressive load. Crippling stress was predicted using Needham's and Gerard's methods — Gerard's was selected as the design basis for its conservatism. Inter-rivet spacing (1.388 in.) was calculated to prevent premature inter-rivet buckling.

SolidWorks FEA simulations were run in 500 lbf increments up to 5,000 lbf. Strain distributions were compared against experimental strain gauge measurements from a hydraulic press and NI DAQ system. The panel withstood loads well beyond the 3,000 lbf target.
Tools & Methods
SolidWorks FEAMATLABStrain GaugesNI DAQGerard's MethodNeedham's Method
Key Findings
  • Gerard's method matched experimental strain most accurately
  • Panel withstood 5,000 lbf with no structural collapse
  • Localized FEA yielding at stiffener edges @ 5k lbf
  • Strain spike attributed to local bending under gauge
  • Two stiffeners effectively doubled theoretical failure load
// Physical Testing
Riveted aluminum stiffened panel with strain gauge bonded to center — pre-test configuration
Panel under hydraulic press — visible buckling deformation at 5,000 lbf
// SolidWorks FEA Results
Static displacement (URES) @ 3,000 lbf — max 3.80 mm at loaded edge
Equivalent strain (ESTRN) @ 3,000 lbf — concentrated along stiffener ribs, within elastic range
Von Mises stress @ 3,000 lbf — max stress below Al yield strength (275 MPa)
Equivalent strain (ESTRN) @ 5,000 lbf — elevated strain at corners and stiffener edges
Von Mises stress @ 5,000 lbf — localized regions exceed Al yield strength (275 MPa), onset of plastic deformation
// Experimental vs Analytical vs Numerical
SolidWorks numerical strain vs load — linear elastic response up to 5,000 lbf
Full comparison — Experimental (blue), SolidWorks (orange), Needham (black dot), Gerard (red dot)
P.03 Aerodynamics MAE 372 — Lab 6

Wing Aerodynamics — Wind Tunnel Testing

Aspect Ratio & Taper Ratio Effects · Low-Speed Wind Tunnel
Experimentally characterized the aerodynamic performance of 3D-printed flat-plate wing models with varying aspect ratios (AR = 2, 3) and taper ratios (λ = 0, 0.5) in NCSU's subsonic wind tunnel. A six-axis ATI Gamma load cell measured forces and moments across angles of attack from −9° to 25° at Reynolds numbers Re = 1.0×10⁵, 1.1×10⁵, and 1.2×10⁵.

Load cell data was coordinate-transformed in MATLAB to extract C_L, C_D, and C_M coefficients after subtracting sting interference. Results reveal clear aerodynamic trade-offs between planform configurations.
Tools & Methods
MATLABSubsonic Wind TunnelATI Gamma Load CellNI USB-6009Velmex Traverse3D Printing
Key Findings
  • Lower AR wings achieve higher C_L max with gradual stall
  • Tapered wings (λ=0.5) show steeper lift slopes & higher C_L max
  • Higher AR increases drag at low Re from tip vortices
  • Reynolds number had minimal impact on comparative trends
  • Tapered wings prone to earlier tip stall at high α
// Aspect Ratio Comparison — AR2 TR0 vs AR3 TR0
C_L vs α @ Re = 1.0×10⁵
C_L vs α @ Re = 1.1×10⁵
C_L vs α @ Re = 1.2×10⁵
C_D vs α @ Re = 1.0×10⁵
C_D vs α @ Re = 1.1×10⁵
C_D vs α @ Re = 1.2×10⁵
C_M vs α @ Re = 1.0×10⁵
C_M vs α @ Re = 1.1×10⁵
C_M vs α @ Re = 1.2×10⁵
// Taper Ratio Comparison — AR3 TR0 vs AR3 TR0.5
C_L vs α — Tapered wing (TR0.5) achieves higher C_L max with steeper lift slope across all Re
C_D vs α — Tapered wing shows lower drag at low Re; tip stall causes drag rise at higher angles
C_M vs α — Tapered wing spikes to higher C_M as center of pressure shifts forward during lift buildup
